bug_summary.title: NullPointerException in UserSessionManager causing silent auth failures
bug_summary.scope: Auth layer — UserSession initialisation in SessionManager.create_session()
symptoms: Login returns HTTP 200 OK but session is not properly initialised
session.user_id is None after successful login
Silent failure — no error shown to the user
~10% failure rate, only under concurrent load
Non-deterministic — not reproducible in sequential dev testing

key_log_evidence: 'session.user_id is None after async init' — 4 warning occurrences
3 AttributeError lines all point to session/manager.py line 83
All failures occur during concurrent login burst at 09:45:00 (5+ simultaneous logins)
Sequential logins at 08:15 and 08:16 succeed with no errors
Error: AttributeError: 'UserSession' object has no attribute '_user_id'
Deployment log confirms v2.3.1 is active on all nodes (last entry: 11:00)

call_chain: Error: _user_id attribute does not exist yet

red_herrings_dismissed: Memory heap at 74% (10:15) — flagged as RED HERRING in log, normal under load
Slow DB query 210ms (09:48) — performance issue, unrelated to session auth bug
Cache eviction at 09:46 — Redis memory pressure, not related to AttributeError
GC cycle 3ms (10:15) — normal garbage collection, not a cause

repro_artifact.expected_output: BUG REPRODUCED — AttributeError on session.user_id, exit code 1
repro_artifact.stdout_preview:

root_cause_hypothesis.hypothesis: Race condition: asyncio.ensure_future() used instead of await in SessionManager.create_session()
root_cause_hypothesis.explanation: In v2.3.1, create_session() was refactored to be async. The session._async_init(user_id) coroutine was scheduled with asyncio.ensure_future() instead of being awaited. ensure_future() returns immediately — it schedules the coroutine but doesn't wait for it to complete. So the session is returned to the caller with _user_id not yet set. When the auth middleware then accesses session.user_id, Python raises AttributeError: 'UserSession' object has no attribute '_user_id'. This only appears under concurrent load because the event loop is busy handling other requests when ensure_future callbacks run.
evidence_references: Log: 'session.user_id is None after async init' — 4 occurrences
Stack trace: AttributeError in session/manager.py line 83
Version delta: v2.3.0 (working, sync) → v2.3.1 (broken, async refactor)

patch_plan.diff:
# ──── BEFORE (buggy v2.3.1) ────────────────────────────────
async def create_session(self, user_id: str) -> UserSession:
    token = str(uuid.uuid4())
    session = UserSession(token)
    asyncio.ensure_future(session._async_init(user_id))  # ← BUG
    self._sessions[token] = session
    return session  # returned before _user_id is set!

# ──── AFTER (fixed) ─────────────────────────────────────────
async def create_session(self, user_id: str) -> UserSession:
    token = str(uuid.uuid4())
    session = UserSession(token)
    await session._async_init(user_id)  # ← FIX: wait for completion
    self._sessions[token] = session
    return session  # _user_id is guaranteed to be set now
additional_hardening: Add '_user_id = None' in UserSession.__init__ to prevent AttributeError before init
Add assert/guard in user_id property: if self._user_id is None: raise ValueError
Add try/except in create_session around _async_init to handle DB write failures
Search entire codebase for other uses of ensure_future for similar init patterns
risks: The await adds ~50ms to login latency (DB write delay). Measure against SLA.
If _async_init raises an exception, create_session will propagate it — ensure caller handles it.

validation_plan.run_repro_after_fix: python output/repro_test.py — should exit 0 (no FAILED entries)
validation_plan.load_test: k6 or locust: 200 concurrent users, 5 minutes, expect 0 AttributeError in logs
regression_checks: All existing auth tests must pass
No 401 errors in prod for 24h post-deploy

critiques.note: Bug reproduced. Repro is minimal, focused, and runnable standalone.
Root cause is specific and backed by log evidence + repro. High confidence (0.95).
await instead of ensure_future is the correct Python async fix. Low risk, well-understood.
MUST add '_user_id = None' in UserSession.__init__ to prevent AttributeError in any early-access scenario.
If _async_init raises a DB exception, create_session will propagate it. Wrap in try/except and handle gracefully.
Adding await adds ~50ms to login path. Measure login p95 before and after fix. Acceptable trade-off for correctness.
Scan entire codebase for other ensure_future() usages — the v2.3.1 async refactor may have introduced similar bugs elsewhere.
Users who hit broken sessions have corrupted auth state. Run DB cleanup to invalidate sessions with NULL user_id.
No concurrent login test existed pre-v2.3.1. This test MUST be added as a required regression test.

edge_cases: What if asyncio event loop is not running when ensure_future is called in tests? (test isolation issue)
What if user_id is valid but empty string ''? Both None and '' should be treated as invalid.
Is session token globally unique? uuid4() is safe but verify no collision handling needed.
Are there broken sessions in production right now with NULL user_id that need cleanup?
Does the session store have a max size? Under load, could sessions pile up before cleanup job runs?
pre_deploy_checklist: Write concurrent login test (50+ simultaneous users)
Run full regression test suite (all green)
Deploy to staging, run load test, verify 0 AttributeError in logs
Run DB cleanup to invalidate broken sessions with NULL user_id
Scan codebase for other ensure_future() usage patterns

open_questions: Are there other ensure_future() usages in the codebase?
Do broken production sessions need a DB cleanup migration?
What is the acceptable login latency budget post-fix?
